This motivated the change making `PointeeSized` effectively syntactic sugar for an absence of any bounds whatsoever. I've added a comment describing this.

comments.author: fee1-dead
comments.body: After the sized hierarchy is introduced, does this _need_ `sized_hierarchy` enabled to work? If yes, that is a potential concern. If not, these tests should remain without using the feature, while you can add the tests to under the experimental feature.

comments.author: davidtwco
comments.body: It is necessary for the `non_lifetime_binders` tests - these tests only work when `for<T>` is unconstrained and with that feature being incomplete, it isn't possible to  add the bounds necessary to keep this test passing w/out the parameters being unconstrained. The only way to make a parameter unconstrained now is with `PointeeSized`, so the feature needs to be enabled.

comments.author: fee1-dead
comments.body: This comment is actually slightly misleading. The function only checks whether the type is a struct. If it returns `None` it could still be a `str` that is not `Sized`. This should really be renamed alongside `adt_sizedness_constraint` to something that better matches what they actually do, something like `opt_trailing_field_ty` or a name to that effect

comments.author: davidtwco
comments.body:
I've rewritten the comments on `sizedness_constraint`, `adt_sizedness_constraint` and `sizedness_constraint_for_ty` but I haven't renamed them. I don't think `opt_trailing_field_ty` is the correct name because the functions do sizedness-specific things.

In the case of this specific function, it wouldn't return `None` for a `str` because this is a function on `AdtDef` and could never be a `str`. This could apply to `adt_sizedness_constraint` or `sizedness_constraint_for_ty` and I've clarified what the result would be in the comments of those.

comments.author: davidtwco

I've also pulled forward a change from Part II that removes `adjust_sizedness_predicate` (where I removed `PointeeSized`) and just puts that in `lower_bounds` to avoid lowering any of those predicates in the first place (in Part II, I also need to migrate `Sized` to `const Sized`, which ended up being another change that made sense in `lower_bounds` to make sure it always happened).

comments.author: davidtwco

comments.author: oli-obk
comments.body: right, the question is why. I don't think your changes should have affected that, so some additional query edges seem to have been added. I wish incremental tests told us why something was dirty 🙃 Would be nice to fix or at least know why

comments.author: davidtwco
comments.body:
Confirmed that this change to the test is definitely still necessary.

My best guess is that I check `tcx.trait_is_auto` now? I need that to avoid adding the default supertrait to things like `Copy`, because if I do that, I can't relax it back with `PointeeSized` unless I do it with a `cfg(bootstrap)` (beta rustc won't allow explicit supertraits on auto traits). This could be fixed later by allowing the sizedness traits to be added to auto traits and then once that is in beta, removing the `trait_is_auto` condition (or doing that now with a `cfg` on all(?) our auto traits). 

Other than that, the only methods called in diff additions in all of `rustc_hir_analysis` on `tcx` are:

- `require_lang_item`
- `is_lang_item`
- `is_default_trait`
- `trait_is_auto`

comments.author: oli-obk

comments.author: davidtwco
comments.body: I didn't, I only changed tests that failed and needed changing. In this test, for example, `?Sized` would no longer be unconstrained, it would be `MetaSized`-constrained, and so the test would be doing something different (and failing as a consequence).

comments.author: davidtwco

I've just blessed these instead in the commit which was changing them.
